item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So toss it in the ocean. Only takes a small group of people

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with many of the concerns and approvals here and have finally settled on a +1. Great title btw.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh damn. I only got as far as "it makes the Foundation lose useful employee(s)". This makes it much cleverer- it's not just a destabilizing strategy, it doubles as a recruitment tool.

Which also suddenly makes it fit better with the Serpent's Hand instead of a group more focused on opposing the Foundation- it isn't about the Foundation, it's about their employees. Instead of wondering "wouldn't this make more sense coming from the CI?" I'm now wondering "wouldn't it make sense for the CI (and the GOC, and the UIU, and the ORIA…) to have something just like this lying around somewhere?". :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The second is to simply take the hit on mindwipes and get it out of Foundation custody in a way that makes it unlikely to come back - space or deep ocean come to mind.

How many people does it take to send something into space? That could be a very big hit…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's about what I was going for, yes. And more specifically, it's one the Foundation has to keep around. They have no idea what'll happen if they intentionally stop containing it (whether by letting an allied GoI take over, or simply via a literal application of the locked box test), and they certainly don't want to risk finding out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…it's a brain drain on the Foundation, isn't it? If someone manages to get it out of containment, it severs their ties to the Foundation, presumably causing them to lose all knowledge they gained in the course of their research there, but otherwise leaves them physically and mentally unharmed. They're still a presumably fairly bright individual with a proven capacity to work with anomalies without constant existential breakdowns or some bullshit like that. If they're a researcher, there's a good chance that they've got a (now latent) interest in infohazards.

I'm sure that most of the people who try to break it out get shot in the head, but if someone does make it out? They're a prime candidate to be recruited and indoctrinated by the SH.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I've been going back through some of my older writing, and I realized that yeah, the tone in the internal communications was indeed a bit odd. The more informal tone of the second pair of messages was intentional, however, although I neglected to indicate why I would have intended it. So that's been edited in. I also adjusted the first pair of messages a bit — I'm going for something of a "these two know each other well enough to make silly jokes" vibe, which I don't think was expressed clearly (or at all) in the initial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno. It's honestly not a bad article at all, though I kind of lost sight of what the addendum was for. That said, it reminds me of the woman who's in containment because some other GoI, forget which, wants to get their hands on her, and the sparkplug (???) that isn't anomalous but is in containment anyway. Neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Although given that SH is highly de-centralised (re: not centralised), one can shove 2414 to the workings of an ultra-radical subgroup of SH with a specific vendetta against the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is better suited for the Chaos Insurgency then. At least they'd have a motive, versus the Hand which does it because… ???

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess I was wrong then, for some reason I was under the impression that a cognitohazard was something that could change your way of thinking (wipe your memory, make you adopt a philosophy, etc.) while an infohazard was something that would have physical effects (i.e. you see it and start growing horns or something)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the idea of using the containment procedures for "training purposes." I'm picturing a bunch of junior researchers writing an MCAT-like exam, coming up with creative solutions to anomalous problems in between standard knowledge-testing and reading comprehension questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Regarding the Serpent's Hand part, the whole concept pretty much relies on some hostile GOI being involved, since it's basically a memetic bomb purpose-built to affect Foundation personnel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reviewing personnel are to be told that SCP-2414 is a fictional SCP object, and that the procedures are to be utilized for training purposes only. Containment procedures presented for review must not refer to SCP-2414 by its actual number; placeholder identifiers such as "SCP-XXXX" are to be used instead.

That's some Ender's Game shit right there.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this idea, but I think you've done too much with it. I understand from the above posts that the mindwipe is integral to the story you're trying to tell, but it feels like a tacked on secondary effect.

What I'd like to see is an item that compels you to believe it's non-anomalous, liberate it from containment, then go find some guy on the sidewalk to give it to… at which point the Foundation employee becomes aware of the fact that they've shown an anomalous object to a member of the public. That'd be a horrifying realization, and it would make more sense with (my understanding of) how the Serpent's Hand operates- they want to bring about awareness and acceptance of the anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've been musing for a while on the idea of a skip which convinces you it's not anomolous, but have repeatedly come to the conclusion that the greatest tribute to such a hazard is that it doesn't have an entry at all.

However, insofar as this can be done well, I think you have done this well. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept (its anomalous property is that makes people believe it's non-anomalous, that is a lovely semi-paradox at work), but I can't see how the Foundation manage to contain it in the first place. Once it was put inside the containment chamber, everyone who knew about it being an anomalous object would've wanted to remove it from Foundation custody. They also would've told anyone who hadn't been informed of the object that it was non-anomalous. And if it only displays this anomalous property when in Foundation custody, why was it brought in in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have been wavering about this one. I like the basic idea (although it's similar to a few other items) and the proposed containment revisions section is a neat illustration, if a bit familiar. I'm also not sure about the Serpent's Hand having a "field team" - I thought they were a bit more amorphous than that. But I'm starting to have concerns about how this skip actually works:

Additionally, SCP-2414 functions as an infohazard that affects Foundation personnel who become aware of its existence as an object considered by the Foundation to be anomalous … personnel unable to [liberate it] will attempt to recruit assistance from those who are.

I think I get this. Senior personnel who were told "the trapezohedron is not anomalous" would not be subject to the infohazard. It's only when they're told "… but the Foundation thinks it is" that the problems start.

Doesn't that lead to a tipping point - if enough senior personnel are affected, then "the Foundation" no longer thinks that it's anomalous, so the infohazard won't spread any further. Of course, by that stage the mind-wipe effect could be problematic, depending on how this next bit is intended:

In the event that SCP-2414 is successfully removed from Foundation custody, all memory and knowledge of the Foundation will be erased from the individual or individuals responsible.

Only the individuals responsible for it being removed from Foundation custody? That would seem to be a relatively small proportion of the number of people infected by the infohazard - it would only take a small subset (albeit including some very senior personnel) to actually cause the release. If the intention was to mindwipe all personnel with any knowledge of SCP-2414, that would be different, but that's not how you've phrased it.

It strikes me that if "considered by the Foundation to be anomalous" is the primer for the infohazard, and release is the trigger for the mindwipe, there are two slightly better ways of containment. One is to make this an -EX, so that the Foundation does not consider it anomalous. The second is to simply take the hit on mindwipes and get it out of Foundation custody in a way that makes it unlikely to come back - space or deep ocean come to mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But this is fridge logic moment when you think of it. They can just say "individuals" in general, since only Foundation people would be aware of it. Maybe they figured out that the SH members are not affected by the anomaly?

But how would the in-universe writer be so certain about its effect to use the terms "affects Foundation personnel" unless said writer knows that the otherwise does not occur?

I know you were going for a "troll the Foundation" thing, but this shatters the narrative by not really sinking into the in-universe perspective. It could be a very cool moment, but it fails to show itself as that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Which would probably still end with the Foundation doing the same thing, albeit with much more urgency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I decided early on that delving too far into "this is how we know it does X" would end up making the SCP too cumbersome, so I guess I never really thought about that. That's not an entirely impractical approach, though, particularly if amnestics get involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
deliver it to some relevant authority figure

Actually, I would imagine it to be more of share on Facebook and tell everyone you have made a discovery unlike any ever before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ In which I explain the motive further
- In which I explain the motive further

It's less that they want the object itself to break out, and more that they want what happens to Foundation personnel who break it out.

It's expected that after its liberation they or someone else will go 'how the hell does this impossible object exist', deliver it to some relevant authority figure, and that eventually the Foundation would find out about it and send people to secure it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's something that popped up while seeing the comments thread:

Additionally, SCP-2414 functions as an infohazard that affects Foundation personnel who become aware of its existence as an object considered by the Foundation to be anomalous.

Does that mean that the Foundation knew that it affects only them? How did they figure it out? Did they test it on non-Foundation people to see the extent of the effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Besides… if it's neutralized, it probably no longer counts as being in Foundation custody.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got the impression that the majority of the MTF secured the item, were affected by the infohazard, removed it from Foundation custody (e.g. immediately threw it into a ditch), and then forgot they were in an MTF.

As far as why not Neutralize it, well, that's generally speaking not something the Foundation voluntarily does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was almost ready to swallow this, even when I found out that the object was some basic item and not something weird or interesting. But the Serpent's Hand connection feels tacky and stretched thin (I'd argue it's better not to know how the Foundation discovered it at all), and the emails feel excessively informal. Some degree of informality can be okay; this isn't.

But above all, when I finished, I ultimately felt like I read a rehash of other objects. 1663 and 1500 immediately came immediately to mind. I like infohazards, but I can't support something that feels really similar to more than one object, and isn't executed in a manner to set them apart. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Due to its properties as an infohazard, all but two members of the MTF succumbed to SCP-2414's effects."

What effects? My understanding of the object is that it will try to convince you it's non-anomalous IF it's in foundation containment, only wiping the foundation from the individuals responsible for freeing it.

Or all but two members viewed it as having nine sides?

I might just not be getting it, but from how it reads to me, the item in only an info hazard while under foundation containment. Which just leaves me wondering why they keep it around at all.

according to the description the faces can be marked through typical methods… so it's not indestructible?

why not neutralize it? Lots of other skips could in theory be destroyed but aren't because the anomaly is worth researching, not cost efficient to destroy, could create a larger problem ect.

But the only way this SCP is dangerous is if the foundation actually keeps it. It literally appears to do nothing else that would really give the foundation a reason to contain it.

All that being said, it's not bad either, I just feel like it needs….something.

An anomalous object which causes containment breaches is a great hook for an object, but it's a hook with no bait.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"We want our weird object to break itself out while effectively disabling Foundation personnel" feels more like an MC&D or CI thing.

I can totally see this as a prototype for some CI scheme to screw the Foundation big-time here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: From Dr. Mackenzie's Glossary, a cognitohazard is something that is dangerous to sense or percieve, whereas an infohazard is something that's dangerous to know about at all. The more you know!

As for the article…. I need to think on this one for a bit. The physical anomaly is extremely basic, but I get that that's not important. I think this is very well thought-out, but I'd also be interested to know why it escapes and then wipes any memory of the Foundation. It seems that it was very specifically targeted at creation to get out of Foundation custody, and I'd like to know who did that and why. The connection that the Serpent's Hand possibly did it… doesn't seem to fit for some reason, for me. I almost feel like they found it that way too. (Edit: I think the reason I feel that way is that it doesn't really fit their motives. "We want our weird object to break itself out while effectively disabling Foundation personnel" feels more like an MC&D or CI thing. If there's more specifics about why the SH made it that way, I would prefer to at least partially know them.) Without a hint of that, this is a bit… sterile.

No vote for now, possible change after some more digestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The connection does make sense, although I do not like how GoIs are used in the "GoI-XXX had this item; the Foundation found said item and keeps it as an SCP". It feels dry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the Serpent's Hand connection makes sense, as this seems like something that might have been created by them specifically to screw with the Foundation.

However, according to my understanding of the terms, this is a cognitohazard, not an infohazard (at least I think so, correct me if I'm wrong).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For one thing, the SH reference is left out in the open as a generic GoI that has said SCP without adding on/hinting on its role with said SCP. A few lines/hints to show a faint tint of connection may help.

Secondly, the e-mails are too informal to be taken seriously. As they are, they feel like an exchange among friends rather than talking about the affairs of their work (which is the context). Since the content of those e-mails are about "training" and handling SCPs, the tone should at least be formal and treat the subject-matter with the seriousness it deserves.

PS. How a procedure modification got screwed over by bureaucratic red tape is at least funny in my eyes. But I'm still downvoting due to informal e-mails that would not fit a work setting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is actually pretty good. Extra kudos to you if this is your first SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Guess it's time to throw my meticulously crafted SCP to the wolves. Hopefully I'll at least manage to go a few hours before hitting negative double digits.

Thanks to MrWrong and Palaios for offering feedback on the draft, as well as the one guy in #site19 whose name I already forgot. (Sorry!)
